languages._comment_pre8: 6/7 kept as reference-only (include:false), unlike Node's 0.10/0.12 which are reference-only but still pullable via archive.debian.org redirects -- Java 6/7 have NO available Docker image at all, live or archived. eclipse-temurin (Adoptium) never built JDK 6/7 (its own baseline starts at 8); the older, now-deprecated 'openjdk' Docker Official Image DID historically ship 6/7/8, but those tags return a 404 today (verified directly against the registry API) -- fully removed, not just unmaintained. No archive mirror or redirect fixes this the way it did for Node/Go's old base images. 8 is therefore the practical floor for this language, not an arbitrary scope cut.
languages._comment_non_lts: Expanded from LTS-only (8,11,17,21,25) to also include every non-LTS feature release from 18 onward (18,19,20,22,23,24), on request for major-version coverage across the board. Verified live against Docker Hub's registry API which non-LTS versions actually have a pullable eclipse-temurin image TODAY: 18/19/20/22 still have a live 'N-jre-jammy' tag; 23/24 moved to Ubuntu noble as their default base ('N-jre-noble', no '-jammy' variant exists for them at all -- confirmed by listing every '23-jre-*'/'24-jre-*' tag) -- see _jdk_os_suffix() in lang_java.py for the per-version dispatch this requires (RUNTIME stage only -- the maven BUILDER image's own OS-suffixed tags turned out to be only inconsistently published across versions, so that stage deliberately stays on its bare untagged default instead), the same class of fix as this project's .NET '-bookworm-slim' dispatch for glibc-sensitive combos. Non-LTS 9/10/12/13/14/15/16 are NOT included -- verified NO exact 'N-jre-jammy' (or any other base-OS variant) tag exists for eclipse-temurin at any of these numbers; Eclipse Temurin as a brand only began publishing images in 2021 (after the Eclipse Foundation took over from AdoptOpenJDK), and JDK 9-16 (2017-2021) were already superseded before Temurin existed. The OLDER, now-deprecated 'adoptopenjdk/openjdkN' Docker Hub repos DO still technically have live tags for 9-16 (confirmed, e.g. adoptopenjdk/openjdk9 still has 109+ tags) -- but under a completely different, JDK-only (no JRE-only slim variant) tag scheme with no bundled-Maven builder image available, which would need a structurally different generator path, not just a tag substitution. A fundamentally larger and riskier scope of work than this project's other reference-only exclusions -- the same category of judgment call as Java 6/7's proprietary-Oracle-JDK dead end.
languages._comment_framework_scope: Every framework major documented on Maven Central is now tracked (the earlier deliberate scope/time cut has been removed) except where noted otherwise below. Vert.x 1.x remains excluded for a STRUCTURAL reason: it was published under a completely different Maven groupId ('org.vert-x', hyphenated) with only 4 releases ever, predating the io.vertx:vertx-core lineage entirely -- not the same kind of gap as the other frameworks' missing majors.
languages._comment_platform_limitations: PLATFORM LIMITATION -- read this first if a build/test fails specifically on an arm64 host (Apple Silicon Mac, ARM Docker runner) but the exact same image builds and passes fine on amd64: Conscrypt (both tracked buckets, '1' and '2') will fail at runtime with an UnsatisfiedLinkError/native-provider-load failure on linux/arm64, because neither bundled version ships ARM64 native libraries at all -- see the 'PLATFORM LIMITATION' tag in the Conscrypt library's own notes field below for the full detail and version numbers checked. This is an upstream Conscrypt gap, not a bug in this generator or in the resulting image -- the same generated Dockerfile is expected to keep failing on arm64 until Conscrypt ships a stable release with ARM64 natives (2.6-alpha5 has them but is still a prerelease). No other framework/library combination in this registry is known to have any platform- or architecture-specific limitation as of this writing -- every other crypto library tracked here is either pure Java/pure-language (JCA, BouncyCastle, Tink) or compiles its native code from source inside the target container at build time (none apply to Java specifically, but this is the pattern followed by the equivalent native libraries in the Python/Go/Node registries too), which is architecture-agnostic by construction rather than by explicit testing.

frameworks.name: Spring Boot
frameworks.notes: Maven coordinate used for resolution is spring-boot-starter-parent (all spring-boot-* artifacts release in lockstep). 1.x's own docs (docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/1.5.x) state Java 7 required, Java 8 recommended -- kept at Java 8 only since that's this project's JDK floor; not verified against 11+ and likely too old to work there (pre-dates javax->jakarta and much else). 2.x's documented floor is Java 8, latest 2.7.x patch tested up to Java 18 -- '18' added on that specific documented ceiling, still not officially validated against 19+/21/25, kept conservative there. 3.x requires Java 17+ throughout the whole line (docs.spring.io/spring-boot/system-requirements.html), no mid-major bump found -- extended to every tracked JDK 17+ (18-24 added alongside the already-tracked LTS 17/21/25) since the documented floor has no stated ceiling; this is floor-based inference for the non-LTS additions specifically, not independently re-verified per JDK the way the milestone facts elsewhere in this registry are. 4.x also requires Java 17+ (docs.spring.io confirms 4.1.0 supports up to Java 26) -- spring-boot-starter-web is deprecated there in favor of spring-boot-starter-webmvc but still resolves and works today (verified against Maven Central metadata), so no template change was needed to add this major; same floor-based extension to 18-24 applied.

frameworks.name: Quarkus
frameworks.notes: Resolution anchor is quarkus-bom (imported with scope=import to keep all io.quarkus:* artifacts on one consistent version, the idiomatic Quarkus setup). 1.x's Java 8 floor confirmed via Quarkus's own blog (the 2.0.0.Final post explicitly tells Java-8 users to stay on 1.13.x). 2.x requires Java 11 (quarkus.io/blog/quarkus-2-0-0-final-released), upper bound kept conservative at 17 since 2.x's actively-maintained window ended well before JDK 21/25 existed -- untested there. Quarkus 3's JDK floor actually jumped mid-line: Java 11 through 3.6, Java 17 from 3.7 (Jan 2024) onward -- since this registry always resolves to the LATEST patch of a tracked major, and latest 3.x is now Java-17-floor, the bucket is set to 17+ (not 11+) to match what's actually installed. Extended to every tracked JDK 17+ (18-24 added alongside the already-tracked LTS 17/21/25) since no ceiling is documented above the 17 floor -- floor-based inference for the non-LTS additions, not independently re-verified per JDK. Quarkus 2's existing 17-ceiling is left UNCHANGED (not extended) -- it reflects a specific, deliberate prior finding (2.x's actively-maintained window ended before JDK 18 even existed), not an oversight to fix. Dependency coordinates differ by major -- see lang_java.py's _quarkus_rest_coord(): 1.x/2.x use the classic quarkus-resteasy + quarkus-resteasy-jackson (both existed since before Quarkus 1.0, still published today, contrary to an earlier assumption that these were era-exclusive), plus the javax.ws.rs namespace (pre-Jakarta rename); 3.x uses quarkus-rest-jackson (the post-3.9 rename of quarkus-resteasy-reactive-jackson) and jakarta.ws.rs. MANIFEST.MF-based version reading is broken since 3.1.0.Final (empty Implementation-Version) -- see lang_java.py for the workaround, applied uniformly across all majors.

frameworks.name: Micronaut
frameworks.notes: Both 1.x and 2.x confirmed Java 8 floor via archived official guides (micronaut-projects.github.io/micronaut-docs-mn1 and -mn2). Non-LTS JDK additions use a LIFECYCLE-WINDOW rule rather than pure floor-based inference, deliberately more conservative than this registry's other frameworks: Micronaut's own major-to-major floor bumps (2->8, 4->17, 5->25) suggest genuine JDK-version sensitivity, not just a documentation nicety, so a JDK is only added to a major's compatibility if it was actually released DURING that major's own active-development window (its own release date through the date it was superseded by the next major). 2.x (superseded 2021-09-30, before JDK 18 existed 2022-03-22) gets NO non-LTS additions -- chronologically impossible for it to have ever run on 18+. 3.x (active 2021-09-30 to 2023-05-30) gains 18/19/20, all released within that window. 4.x (active 2023-05-30 to 5.0's 2026-04-27) gains 22/23/24 (NOT 18/19/20, which predate 4.0's own release and belong to 3.x's window instead). 5.x's Java 25 baseline confirmed via an actual release blog (micronaut.io/2026/04/27/micronaut-framework-5-0-with-java-25-baseline) -- stronger sourcing than the GitHub-discussion-only claim this registry previously relied on. Parent-POM setup differs sharply by major (see _micronaut_parent_coord() / _pom_micronaut() in lang_java.py): io.micronaut:micronaut-parent has ZERO 1.x releases (starts at 2.0.0) -- Micronaut 1.x projects used io.micronaut:micronaut-bom via <dependencyManagement> import instead of a <parent> at all, which also means annotation processing (micronaut-inject-java) must be wired manually via the compiler plugin's annotationProcessorPaths rather than inherited from a parent. 2.x uses io.micronaut:micronaut-parent (not the io.micronaut.platform groupId used from 4.x onward -- 3.x already established this groupId split; 2.x is a THIRD distinct case). micronaut-serde-jackson has zero 1.x/2.x/3.x-era releases (its own versioning starts 2022, after Micronaut 3.0) -- 1.x/2.x rely on Jackson support bundled directly in micronaut-http/micronaut-core, no separate serde dependency at all.

frameworks.name: Vert.x
frameworks.notes: vertx-web added alongside vertx-core for real path-based routing (Router) -- bare vertx-core only gives a low-level HttpServer with no routing (present from 2.x onward too). 2.x's Java 7 floor is confirmed via its actual build.gradle at tag v2.1.6 (sourceCompatibility 1.7) -- kept at Java 8 only in this registry since Java 7 isn't a tracked JDK here, and 2.x is old enough that 11+ compatibility wasn't verified. 3.x confirmed Java 8 floor via vertx-core:3.0.0's own parent POM; upper bound kept conservative at 17 (3.x's actively-maintained window predates 21/25). 4.x kept a Java 8 floor for its entire line; 5.x raised it to Java 11 (Java 21 is only needed opportunistically, for virtual threads). Both 4.x and 5.x extended to every tracked non-LTS JDK (18-24) alongside their existing LTS entries -- floor-based inference (no ceiling documented for either), not independently re-verified per JDK. Vert.x 1.x is NOT tracked (see the language-level _comment_framework_scope) -- it lived under the org.vert-x groupId, a structurally different, long-dead lineage, not a scope cut.

frameworks.name: Helidon
frameworks.notes: Helidon SE flavor (functional/programmatic routing) throughout, not MP (JAX-RS/CDI) -- SE has no annotation-processing/bean-discovery startup overhead and needs no beans.xml. JDK floors confirmed against the official docs-as-code source at each version's own branch: 1.x = 'Java 8 or Java 11', 2.x = 'Java 11 or newer', 3.x = Java 17 (breaking change from 2.x), 4.x = Java 21 (Helidon 4's Nima core is built on virtual threads, mandatory not optional). The WebServer API itself has THREE distinct shapes across these majors, not just one break at 3.x as previously assumed (see _helidon_api_style() in lang_java.py): 1.x uses the static factory WebServer.create(routing) where routing comes from an explicitly-built Routing.builder()....build(), and blocks on startup via a raw CompletableFuture; 2.x also uses WebServer.create(...) but accepts an un-built Routing.Builder directly and blocks via Helidon's own Single.await(); 3.x/4.x use the fluent WebServer.builder().routing(...).build().start() shape this project's template was originally written against. All three confirmed against real code snippets in Helidon's own docs at each version's branch, and each variant was build-and-run verified in a real container, not just compiled. Non-LTS JDK additions use the same lifecycle-window rule as Micronaut (see that framework's notes): a JDK is added to a major's compatibility only if it was released during that major's own active-development window. 2.x (active 2020-09-25 to 3.0's 2022-11-16) gains 18/19. 3.x (active 2022-11-16 to 4.0's 2024-03-19) gains 20 (22 released the SAME DAY as Helidon 4 -- assigned to 4.x instead, not double-counted). 4.x (active since 2024-03-19, no successor yet) gains 22/23/24.

frameworks.name: Javalin
frameworks.notes: Lightweight embedded-Jetty router, no annotations, single Main.java. TWO distinct routing-API shapes, not one unchanged surface across all 5 majors: 3.x-6.x's `Javalin` class directly implements the routing interface, so `app.get(path, handler)` works immediately after `Javalin.create()` -- confirmed live via real javap/source inspection of 5.x and 6.0.0 (`class Javalin implements JavalinDefaultRoutingApi<Javalin>` in 6.0.0). 7.x REMOVED that direct implementation -- confirmed the hard way: an assumed `app.get(...)` call fails to compile ('cannot find symbol') on 7.2.2, because routes must now be registered inside the `Javalin.create(cfg -> cfg.routes.get(path, handler))` config consumer instead. Also, unlike some earlier Javalin lines, 7.x does NOT bundle a JSON object mapper -- `ctx.json(...)` throws a real HTTP 500 at request time ('It looks like you don't have an object mapper configured') unless `jackson-databind` is added as an explicit separate dependency; confirmed via a real docker run, not just a build. Majors 3/4 are tracked as real buckets sharing 5/6's confirmed direct-call template, but were not independently re-verified with their own build this pass. Major 5's floor was wrong -- '8' was listed but Javalin 5 actually dropped Java 8 support at the very start of the line: a real docker build failed with 'class file has wrong version 55.0, should be 52.0' (55=Java 11, 52=Java 8) compiling against the resolved 5.6.5 jar; confirmed via direct class-file-bytecode-version inspection (not just the one failing patch) that even 5.0.0, the FIRST 5.x release, is already compiled to bytecode 55 (Java 11), while 4.6.8 (the LAST 4.x release) is still bytecode 52 (Java 8) -- the floor bump lands exactly at the 5.0 major boundary, not mid-line. Narrowed to `["11", "17", "18", "19"]`.

cryptography_libs.name: JCA
cryptography_libs.notes: Java Cryptography Architecture -- the JDK's own java.security/javax.crypto packages, always available, no Maven dependency. Provider name/version read via java.security.Security.getProviders().

cryptography_libs.name: BouncyCastle
cryptography_libs.notes: Expanded from a 4-bucket pinned-milestone design to EVERY real bcprov-jdk18on minor release (1.71 through 1.84, plus the pre-rename '1.70' legacy bucket -- 15 buckets total), on request for major-version coverage across the board; each minor bump is a real, dated release with genuine content changes, not an incidental patch, so collapsing them into milestones-only was undercounting real history the same way this project's .NET BouncyCastle.Cryptography registry originally did before its own expansion. All dates confirmed live via search.maven.org's solrsearch API for 1.71 through 1.80 (that index is otherwise known to lag Maven Central for the very newest releases -- see below) and via repo1.maven.org's Last-Modified header for 1.81 through 1.84, which the solr index hadn't caught up to yet at research time (confirmed it topped out at 1.80, matching this registry's own long-standing 'solr lags by months to over a year' finding). The dropped 'rolling latest' bucket (previously plain '1') is now redundant with '1.84' explicitly tracking the current latest -- consistent with this project's .NET BC expansion, which dropped its own rolling bucket for the same reason. See _bc_coord() in lang_java.py for the per-bucket artifact switch (still needed only for the '1.70' legacy bucket, which alone resolves to bcprov-jdk15on rather than bcprov-jdk18on): '1.71' (2022-04-06) is bcprov-jdk18on's FIRST release, predating any post-quantum support at all. '1.72' (2022-09-25) is the first release with DRAFT post-quantum support (Kyber/Dilithium, flagged experimental in BC's own release notes) -- confirmed by diffing actual jar contents, 1.71 has none of these classes. Buckets 1.73 through 1.78 are assumed to INHERIT 1.72's draft-PQC support (not independently jar-diffed release-by-release the way the milestone versions were) -- a defensible inference given the confirmed timeline, flagged as slightly lower-confidence than the milestone facts themselves, the same honesty pattern used for .NET BC's 2.1-2.4 buckets. '1.79' (2024-10-30) is where the FINAL NIST-standardized names landed (ML-KEM/ML-DSA/FIPS 203+204, SLH-DSA/FIPS 205) -- confirmed the same way (1.78 lacks MLKEMParameterSpec/MLDSAParameterSpec/SLHDSAParameterSpec entirely, 1.79 introduces full provider implementations for all three while keeping the old draft Kyber/Dilithium names side-by-side for compatibility). Buckets 1.80 through 1.84 inherit 1.79's final-name support (same inheritance caveat as above) -- note BC is actively sunsetting the draft wrapper names starting at 1.84 (1.84's release notes announce their removal in a future release), so the gap between the draft-era buckets (1.72-1.78) and the final-name buckets (1.79+) will read as more consequential over time, not less.

cryptography_libs.name: Tink
cryptography_libs.notes: Expanded from a 2-bucket (milestone + rolling-latest) design to EVERY real Tink Java minor release (1.0 through 1.22, 23 buckets total), on request for major-version coverage across the board -- this also surfaces genuine migration-path data the milestone-only design hid, like the exact Java-8-drop point. Dates confirmed live: 1.0-1.18 via search.maven.org's solrsearch API (that index's well-documented lag for the newest releases didn't affect these, all long since superseded); 1.19/1.20 via repo1.maven.org's Last-Modified header (solr hadn't caught up to them at research time); 1.21/1.22 already established from this registry's own prior milestone research. The dropped 'rolling latest' bucket (previously plain '1') is now redundant with '1.22' explicitly tracking the current latest -- consistent with this project's .NET/Java BouncyCastle expansions dropping their own rolling buckets for the same reason. Tink dropped Java 8 support at 1.19.0 (per official GitHub release notes, already an established fact in this registry before this expansion) -- 1.0 through 1.18 include Java 8 in their compatibility, 1.19 onward excludes it, the exact transition point this wider bucket set now makes visible instead of asserting as a single fact in prose. '1.21' remains Tink's FIRST post-quantum release (ML-DSA-87 signature support, confirmed via full-text search of every Tink Java release body on GitHub -- v1.8.0 through v1.20.0 have zero PQC mentions). '1.22' adds SLH-DSA and ML-DSA-44 on top of 1.21's ML-DSA-87. Tink still has NO KEM-side PQC (no ML-KEM/Kyber) as of 1.22.0 -- only signatures (ML-DSA, SLH-DSA); its hybrid/KEM package remains classical-only (ECIES, HPKE). Buckets 1.0-1.20's JDK compatibility beyond their own already-researched floor is floor-based inference (Tink is pure Java bytecode with no known JPMS/reflection sensitivities), not independently re-verified per JDK -- same honesty caveat as this registry's other newly-expanded buckets.

cryptography_libs.name: Conscrypt
cryptography_libs.notes: Expanded from a 2-bucket (legacy-line + current-line rolling-latest) design to every real minor across BOTH lines (1.0-1.4 and 2.0-2.2/2.4/2.5, 10 buckets total -- note Conscrypt's OWN numbering has a genuine gap, no 2.3 was EVER released, confirmed via Maven Central metadata, not an omission in this registry), on request for major-version coverage across the board. '2.6' is kept as an available:false REFERENCE row -- every 2.6 release to date (alpha through alpha5) is a prerelease, filtered out by the same stable-only version regex used everywhere else in this project; re-flip once a real 2.6.0 stable lands. Dates confirmed live via search.maven.org's solrsearch API (Conscrypt's full history was fully indexed there, unlike BC/Tink's newest releases -- no lag encountered). Bundles native code (glibc-linked per standard OpenJDK toolchain convention) -- all Java Docker images in this project use eclipse-temurin's Ubuntu/glibc ('-jammy'/'-noble') tags rather than Alpine/musl specifically to avoid a suspected (not yet empirically confirmed) UnsatisfiedLinkError. '1.4' resolves to 1.4.2, the highest published 1.x release -- a genuinely legacy, superseded line (unlike BouncyCastle/Tink where only one line ever existed), included here because it's still resolvable today and the user asked for old-but-buildable versions. PLATFORM LIMITATION found while researching the original 2-bucket version of this entry, unrelated to the version split itself and still fully applicable to the new buckets: neither the 1.x line (checked 1.4.2, now bucket '1.4') nor the current stable 2.x line (checked 2.5.2, now bucket '2.5') bundles ARM64/aarch64 native libraries at all -- only the still-prerelease 2.6-alpha5 adds those. Conscrypt WILL fail to load its native provider (UnsatisfiedLinkError, or a similar native-load failure surfaced through Security.addProvider(Conscrypt.newProvider()) in this project's blank-import/'touch' line) on an arm64 host -- Apple Silicon Mac, ARM Docker Desktop, ARM CI runner -- with buckets '1.4' or '2.5' specifically confirmed; the other newly-added buckets (1.0-1.3, 2.0-2.2, 2.4) are PRESUMED to share the same gap (same native-library lineage, same build toolchain) but have NOT been independently jar-inspected one by one. If a build/test for this exact library fails on arm64 but the identical image builds and passes on amd64, this is the expected, already-known cause -- not a bug in the generator, not something to re-diagnose from scratch. This project's own builds target amd64 so it hasn't been hit directly, but it will reproduce deterministically the moment this image is built on an arm64 host. Re-check this note once Conscrypt ships a stable (non-alpha) release with ARM64 natives -- 2.6-alpha5 already has them, just not yet GA.
